learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Two Pointers, Sorting, Loops, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine combining two sorted lists of exam scores into one larger sorted list without using extra storage. Since nums1 already has extra empty space at the end, fill it from the back to avoid overwriting values.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine combining two sorted lists of exam scores into one larger sorted list without using extra storage. Since nums1 already has extra empty space at the end, fill it from the back to avoid overwriting values.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use the Reverse Two Pointers pattern whenever one sorted array has enough extra space to merge another sorted array in-place.
learning.signals: Whenever one sorted array has enough extra space to hold another sorted array, think of Reverse Two Pointers.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: You are given two integer arrays nums1 and nums2 sorted in non-decreasing order, along with integers m and n representing the number of valid elements in each array. Merge nums2 into nums1 as one sorted array. The final sorted array should be stored inside nums1.

examples.explanation: Merge nums2 into nums1 while maintaining sorted order.
nums2 is empty, so nums1 remains unchanged.

edgeCases: All elements of nums2 are smaller
All elements of nums2 are larger

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Merge both arrays into a new array, then copy the result back into nums1.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: The largest element always belongs at the end of the merged array. Compare the largest remaining elements from both arrays and place the larger one at the last available position in nums1.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: The largest remaining element always belongs at the last available position.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever one sorted array has enough extra space to hold another sorted array, think of Reverse Two Pointers.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Filling from the end avoids overwriting valid elements in nums1. Every element is processed exactly once, resulting in linear time and constant extra space.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever one sorted array has enough extra space to hold another sorted array, think of Reverse Two Pointers.
commonWrongPaths: Merging from the front and overwriting values.
Forgetting to copy remaining nums2 elements.
Using extra space despite the in-place requirement.

hints: Copy remaining nums2 elements if necessary.
commonMisconceptions: Merging from the front and overwriting values.
Forgetting to copy remaining nums2 elements.
Using extra space despite the in-place requirement.

pattern.name: Reverse Two Pointers
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever one sorted array has enough extra space to hold another sorted array, think of Reverse Two Pointers.

approaches.title: Merge Using Extra Array
approaches.idea: Merge both arrays into a new array, then copy the result back into nums1.
algorithm: Copy the merged array into nums1.
approaches.dryRun: Merge into an auxiliary array first.

approaches.title: Forward Merge with Shifting
approaches.idea: Merge elements from the front by shifting existing elements whenever necessary.
algorithm: Shift elements in nums1 when required.

approaches.title: Reverse Two Pointers
approaches.idea: Maintain three pointers: i at the last valid element of nums1, j at the last element of nums2, and k at the last position of nums1. Place the larger element at nums1[k] and move the corresponding pointer backward.
approaches.intuition: Filling from the end avoids overwriting valid elements in nums1. Every element is processed exactly once, resulting in linear time and constant extra space.
algorithm: Place the larger value at nums1[k].
Copy remaining nums2 elements if any.

steps: Place the larger value at the end.

interviewFollowUps: Why merge from the end instead of the front?
Why don't we copy remaining nums1 elements?
What if nums1 had no extra space?
How would you merge k sorted arrays?
interviewTips: Explain why merging from the front fails.
Explain why remaining nums1 elements stay in place.
State O(m+n) time and O(1) space.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: The largest remaining element always belongs at the last available position.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Three Pointers → Compare → Fill from Back.

commonMistakes: Merging from the front and overwriting values.
Forgetting to copy remaining nums2 elements.
Using extra space despite the in-place requirement.

youtube: Take U Forward - Merge Sorted Array
relatedProblems: Kth Largest Element in an Array
